name: Samarium
name_origin: Named after the mineral samarskite, itself named for Russian mining engineer Vasili Samarsky-Bykhovets.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Moderately abundant lanthanide (~7 ppm in crust); occurs in monazite, bastnaesite, samarskite, cerite, and gadolinite ores.

compounds.name: Samarium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Pale yellow solid used as a neutron-absorbing additive in glass and ceramics, and as a starting material for SmCo magnets.

compounds.name: Samarium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Bright yellow salt used as a precursor to other samarium chemistry and in catalysis research.

compounds.name: Samarium(II) iodide
compounds.summary: Dark blue solution in THF (Kagan's reagent) is a powerful one-electron reductant in organic synthesis.

compounds.name: Samarium-cobalt (1:5)
compounds.summary: Hexagonal intermetallic compound used as a high-coercivity permanent magnet with excellent thermal stability.

uses: Samarium-cobalt (SmCo) high-temperature permanent magnets for motors and aerospace applications
Sm-Co magnets in headphones, small motors, and traveling wave tubes
Neutron-absorbing control rods in pressurised water nuclear reactors
Samarium iodide (SmI2) as a single-electron reducing agent in organic chemistry
Carbon-arc lighting for the motion picture industry
Optical and infrared absorbing glasses; doping for masers and lasers
reactivity_summary: Silvery-white moderately reactive metal that tarnishes in air to form a yellowish oxide layer; reacts slowly with cold water and rapidly with hot water and acids. Spontaneously ignites in air above ~150 degrees C.
isotopes_summary: Samarium has seven naturally occurring isotopes (Sm-144, Sm-147, Sm-148, Sm-149, Sm-150, Sm-152, Sm-154). Sm-152 is the most abundant (~26.75%); Sm-147, Sm-148 and Sm-149 are very long-lived alpha emitters. Sm-149 is also a strong neutron absorber.
